questions.question: Which operator overloading method would you implement to make instances of your class support the + operator?
choices: __mul__
__add__
__sub__
__mod__
questions.explanation: The __add__ method is called when the + operator is used with an instance on the left side. For example, x + y calls x.__add__(y).

questions.question: To make an instance callable like a function (e.g., obj()), you define the ________ method in the class.
questions.explanation: The __call__ method is invoked when an instance is called using function call syntax. If a class defines __call__, then obj(args) is equivalent to obj.__call__(args).

questions.question: What does the __getitem__ method allow you to do with an instance?
choices: Get the length of the instance
Access the instance using indexing syntax like obj[key]
Iterate over the instance
Check if a value is contained in the instance
questions.explanation: __getitem__ is called when you use indexing syntax (obj[key]) on an instance. It allows your class to support bracket-based access like lists and dictionaries. It can also serve as a fallback for iteration if __iter__ is not defined.

questions.question: Which method must you implement to make your class work with the len() built-in function?
choices: __size__
__count__
__len__
__length__
questions.explanation: __len__ is the special method called by the built-in len() function. It should return an integer representing the logical length or size of the object.

questions.question: To support the 'in' operator for membership testing (e.g., x in obj), you define the ________ method.
questions.explanation: __contains__ is called by the 'in' operator. It should return True or False. If __contains__ is not defined, Python falls back to iterating through the object using __iter__ or __getitem__.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the __bool__ method?
choices: It converts the object to a binary number
It defines the truth value of an instance when used in boolean contexts like if statements
It checks whether two objects are equal
It validates that the object was created correctly
questions.explanation: __bool__ is called when an object is tested in a boolean context (e.g., if obj:, while obj:, bool(obj)). It should return True or False. If __bool__ is not defined, Python falls back to __len__ (nonzero length means True).

questions.question: If a class defines __iter__, what must the __iter__ method return?
choices: A list of all items
An iterator object (an object with a __next__ method)
The number of items in the collection
A boolean indicating whether iteration is possible
questions.explanation: __iter__ must return an iterator object -- that is, an object that implements __next__ (and typically __iter__ returning self). Each call to __next__ should return the next value or raise StopIteration when exhausted.
